title: Kundalini and Tinnitus: The Ringing in Your Ears That Won't Stop

body:
Kundalini and Tinnitus: The Ringing in Your Ears That Won't Stop

You’re here because you’re scared. You’re lying in bed, probably, or maybe you’re pacing. It’s dark. The world’s quiet, but your head isn’t. There’s that sound. That goddamn ringing. And you’ve probably been told it’s just tinnitus, nothing to worry about, just live with it. But you know it’s more than that, don’t you? You feel it. You feel something else happening, something big and scary, and this ringing is just another symptom of the chaos inside you. I know that feeling. I’ve lived it.

I’m not gonna sugarcoat this. What you’re going through, it’s hell. It’s isolating. It’s terrifying. And when you mention "kundalini" to most doctors, you get blank stares or maybe even a referral to a shrink who thinks you're delusional. You're not. You're just experiencing something that traditional Western medicine isn't equipped to deal with. Not yet, anyway. But here's the good news - you're not alone. And it can get better. It really can.

The Sound of Awakening - Or Is It?

Let's talk about that ringing. That high-pitched whine, that hum, that hiss, that buzz. For many, it's the soundtrack to their spiritual awakening, or what feels like one. You hear stories of yogis and meditators talking about the "nada sound" or the "inner sound current," a sign of spiritual progress. And maybe it is, for some. But for you, right now, it’s probably just a constant, maddening intrusion. It's not peaceful, it's not blissful, it's just... loud.

When kundalini starts to move - and for many of us, it doesn't just "rise gracefully," it explodes like a goddamn wildfire - it can mess with your entire system. Your nervous system, your energetic system, your very perception of reality. Tinnitus, or what feels like it, is a super common physical symptom. It’s not just in your ears; it feels like it’s in your head, your bones, sometimes your whole damn body. It’s like an energetic pressure cooker. Know what I mean?

I remember being convinced I was going deaf. Or that I had a brain tumor. Or that I was losing my mind. The sound was so intense, so relentless. It would change pitch, change volume. Sometimes it was a single tone, sometimes a chorus of buzzing. It felt like my skull was vibrating from the inside out. I couldn't escape it. Sleep was impossible. Quiet rooms were torture. It amplified every other terrifying symptom I was experiencing - the involuntary movements, the burning sensations, the extreme anxiety, the feeling of being utterly disconnected from my own body and the world around me.

You’re probably thinking, "Is this forever?" Honestly, I can't give you a definitive "no." But I can tell you what I've seen, what I've experienced, and what others who've walked this path have found. It changes. It fluctuates. And you learn to cope. You learn to integrate. And sometimes, it lessens. Sometimes, it even disappears for periods. It's not a death sentence, even though it feels like one right now.

Why is this happening?

Look, there are a lot of theories. From an energetic perspective, it's often seen as the sound of energy moving through your crown chakra, or your third eye, or your entire nervous system. It's the sound of blockages being cleared, or of new neural pathways being formed. It’s the sound of your system recalibrating. Think of it like a really loud, persistent hum from a massive electrical transformer that's just been switched on for the first time in your head. It’s a lot of power. And your system isn't used to it.

From a more conventional perspective, intense stress, anxiety, and nervous system dysregulation can absolutely cause or worsen tinnitus. And let's be real, kundalini awakening - especially an unmanaged one, a crisis - is the ultimate stressor. Your body is in fight-or-flight mode pretty much constantly. Your adrenal glands are probably shot. Your entire system is screaming for help. This constant state of alert can create physical symptoms like tinnitus, muscle tension, digestive issues, and a whole host of other fun stuff.

It's a chicken and egg thing sometimes. Is the kundalini causing the tinnitus? Or is the extreme nervous system reaction to kundalini causing the tinnitus? Does it even matter? What matters is how you deal with it, how you survive it, and how you eventually integrate it. Right?

Surviving the Noise: Practical Steps

You can't just wish it away. I tried. Believe me, I tried. I begged. I pleaded. I screamed into pillows. None of it worked. But there are things you can do to manage it, to lessen its intensity, and to give yourself some damn peace.

  • Don't fight it: This is probably the hardest advice to take. Your natural instinct is to resist, to push it away. But what you resist, persists. Try to shift your relationship with the sound. Instead of seeing it as an enemy, try to see it as a signal. A weird, annoying, incredibly loud signal. When you tense up against it, you just increase it. Relax into it, even for a second. Breathe.
  • Sound masking: This isn't a cure, but it's a lifesaver. White noise, pink noise, nature sounds - whatever works for you. I used to fall asleep to rain sounds on repeat. Sometimes it was the only thing that gave my brain a break from the internal ringing. Get a sound machine, download an app. Don't be a hero. Use external sound to blend with the internal sound. It doesn't make it go away, but it can make it less noticeable, less jarring.
  • Earplugs (sometimes): This might sound counterintuitive, but sometimes in a noisy environment, earplugs can actually help reduce the external noise, making the internal noise feel less overwhelmed and chaotic. It's about finding a balance.
  • Mindfulness and Acceptance: This is where the spiritual stuff comes back around. If you can sit with the sound, observe it without judgment, without attaching a story to it ("This is going to drive me insane," "I can't live like this"), you can reduce its power. It's not easy. It takes practice. But acceptance isn't about liking it; it's about acknowledging it's there and not fighting it.
  • Grounding practices: This is HUGE. Kundalini is often very "up" energy. You need to pull that energy down, ground yourself. Walk barefoot on the earth. Eat nourishing, heavy foods. Do gentle yoga or tai chi. Spend time in nature. Anything that connects you to the physical world, to your body, and away from the frenetic energy in your head.
  • Diet: Cut out stimulants. Coffee, sugar, alcohol. I know, I know. You're probably already barely functioning. But these things can make your nervous system even more agitated, which can worsen tinnitus. Try it for a week or two and see if it makes a difference.
  • Sleep hygiene: This is probably a joke to you right now. But do what you can. Dark room, cool temperature, no screens before bed. If you can't sleep, don't just lie there in agony. Get up, do something quiet, then try again.

When to Seek Help (The Conventional Kind)

Look, I'm not a doctor. I'm just someone who's been through it. And while I believe this is a spiritual experience, it doesn't mean you should ignore your physical health. Go to an ENT (ear, nose, and throat doctor). Get your hearing checked. Rule out any other physical causes. Get your blood pressure checked. Talk to your primary care doctor. Tell them about the tinnitus, even if you don't mention "kundalini" right away. Get the physical stuff checked out. It might not be "just" kundalini. It might be a combination of things. And even if it is kundalini, having a clean bill of health from a doctor can give you some peace of mind, which is precious right now.

You might also consider a therapist who understands trauma or spiritual emergencies. They're rare, but they exist. Someone who won't immediately dismiss your experiences as psychosis. Sometimes just having someone listen without judgment can be incredibly healing.

The Long Game: Integration, Not Eradication

Here's the truth: you're probably not going to "get rid" of kundalini once it's active. It's not a switch you can turn off. It's a process. And the goal isn't to make it go away, but to integrate it. To learn to live with this heightened state of awareness, this increased energy, and yes, even the ringing. It sounds impossible right now, I know. But it's not.

I've found these books incredibly helpful during my own crisis, and I recommend them to anyone going through something similar. They help you understand what's happening and that you're not crazy:

  • For a general overview of what kundalini can feel like, The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) is a classic. It’s older, but it describes so many of the wild physical and psychological symptoms.
  • The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) is another really good one. She talks about the different stages and how to manage the process.
  • If you're feeling completely overwhelmed and like you're losing your mind, The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) is a lifesaver. It normalizes what you're experiencing as a "spiritual emergency" rather than a mental illness.
  • And the book that really put a name to it for me, and for so many others: Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link). It’s a collection of essays and it just makes you feel seen.

They won't make the ringing stop, but they'll give you context. They'll tell you you're not alone. And that's a huge part of the battle right now.

What you're experiencing is a deep shift. It's messy. It's uncomfortable. It's not a gentle, blissful awakening for everyone. For many of us, it's a brutal, disorienting upheaval. The ringing is just one piece of that puzzle. It's your system screaming for balance. It's your consciousness expanding in ways you can't yet comprehend. It's a reminder that something big is happening inside you.

I'm not gonna lie and say it's easy. It's not. There will be days when you feel like you can't take it anymore. Days when the ringing is so loud you can't hear your own thoughts. But there will also be moments of peace. Moments where you realize you're stronger than you ever thought possible. Moments where you connect with something deeper, something true, beyond the noise.

You're in the thick of it. And it's okay to be scared. It's okay to be angry. It's okay to feel completely lost. Just keep breathing. Keep trying the small things that might give you a moment's relief. And remember, you're not crazy. You're just experiencing something that's pushing the boundaries of what you thought was possible. Hold on. You've got this.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
